1:
"After fourteen years" — the chronology is debated. If reckoned from Paul's conversion (c. AD 34), fourteen years brings us to approximately AD 48; if reckoned from his first Jerusalem visit (c. AD 37), it arrives at approximately AD 51. Most scholars identify this visit with the Jerusalem Council of Acts 15 (c. AD 48-49). The "again" (πάλιν) indicates this is his second Jerusalem visit since conversion — a data point that matters for identifying the Acts counterpart.

Barnabas was a Levite from Cyprus, highly respected in Jerusalem as one of the community's first generous donors (Acts 4:36-37) and the one who initially vouched for Paul after his conversion (Acts 9:27). His presence would have lent credibility to Paul's delegation. Titus, a Gentile Christian, served as a deliberate test case for the uncircumcised-Gentile question.
2: "I went in response to a revelation" — Paul did not go because he was summoned or because he felt his gospel needed validation. The explicit insistence on divine prompting (ἀποκάλυψις) mirrors his account of his original call: Paul acts by divine directive, not human invitation or political calculation. The "private meeting" with the leaders before the larger assembly reflects ancient political and legal practice — preliminary bilateral discussion before formal proceedings prevented embarrassing public confrontations and gave both parties space to establish common ground.
3: Titus's case is pivotal: a Gentile believer, uncircumcised, whom the Jerusalem leaders accepted without requiring circumcision. Paul presents this as the implicit verdict of the Jerusalem pillars — their refusal to compel Titus to be circumcised was a de facto recognition of the law-free Gentile mission. The historical significance: if even at the Jerusalem Council no one required Titus's circumcision, the Judaizers' later insistence on it in Galatia is an innovation, not a tradition.
4: The language is military and legal: παρείσακτοι ("those who came in stealthily/secretly") is used of infiltrators who enter under false pretenses; κατασκοπέω ("to spy out") is a reconnaissance term. Paul describes the Judaizers not as sincere Christians with different views but as hostile agents who entered the community to surveil its freedom and bring it under law's control. "Slavery" is the outcome Paul warns against — not metaphorical bondage but the concrete condition of living under the law's totalizing demand.
5: "We did not give in to them for a moment" — the resistance at Jerusalem was a matter of principle, not stubbornness. Paul frames the debate at the Council in terms of "the truth of the gospel" as something entrusted to be preserved for later Gentile churches — including the Galatians. What was decided in Jerusalem directly affects what the Galatians now owe: the Jerusalem verdict against compulsory circumcision is the heritage Paul is defending in this letter.
6: "Those held in high esteem" — οἱ δοκοῦντες ("those reputed / those who seem to be something") is a phrase Paul uses three times in this chapter. The repetition is not contemptuous; Paul acknowledges the pillars' status while insisting it does not affect his argument. "Whatever they were makes no difference to me; God does not show favoritism" — the appeal to divine impartiality (echoing Deuteronomy 10:17) is Paul's way of saying: the truth of the gospel does not vary by who endorses it. The Jerusalem leaders "added nothing to my message" — ἐμοὶ γὰρ οἱ δοκοῦντες οὐδὲν προσανέθεντο means they appended no supplements, no additional requirements, no corrections.
7: The formal recognition of two missions — Paul's to the uncircumcised (Gentiles), Peter's to the circumcised (Jews) — was not a permanent territorial division but a practical acknowledgment of where each was already working effectively. The language of "entrusted with the task" (πεπίστευμαι) uses the perfect passive of πιστεύω (believe/entrust) — a divine commissioning given in trust. Both missions share the same gospel; the difference is ethnic audience, not theological content.
8: "God, who was at work in Peter as an apostle to the circumcised" — the language of God working ἐνεργήσας (energizing, being at work) in both Peter and Paul parallels their apostolic effectiveness. The argument is symmetric: the same God producing the same kind of visible results in both missions vindicates both commissions equally. For Paul's Galatian argument, this symmetry means his apostleship is not inferior to Peter's; the Galatians have received a gospel of equal authority.
9:
"Those reputed to be pillars" (στύλοι) — the pillar image was used in Second Temple Judaism for foundational figures of the covenant community; the Temple itself had its defining pillars (Jachin and Boaz, 1 Kings 7:21). Applied to James, Cephas, and John, it designates them as the architectural support of the early church.

The "right hand of fellowship" (δεξιὰς κοινωνίας) was a formal covenant or treaty gesture in the ancient Near East and Hellenistic world — an act of legal partnership and mutual recognition. It bound the parties to a specific arrangement: Paul and Barnabas to the Gentiles, the Jerusalem leaders to the circumcised. The handshake was not casual but constitutive of a formal agreement.
10: "Continue to remember the poor" — the "poor" here most likely refers specifically to the Jerusalem church, which experienced genuine material poverty (Acts 11:27-30; Romans 15:25-27). The collection for Jerusalem occupied a significant portion of Paul's later ministry (2 Cor 8–9; Rom 15). The request was "the very thing I had been eager to do all along" — Paul presents material generosity to Jerusalem as something he already embraced, not a new obligation imposed on him.
11:
The Antioch incident (Galatians 2:11-14) is the most dramatic confrontation in Paul's letters. Antioch-on-the-Orontes was the third largest city of the Roman Empire after Rome and Alexandria, a cosmopolitan hub where Jews, Syrians, Greeks, and Romans mixed. The city was where followers of Jesus were first called "Christians" (Acts 11:26) and where the mixed Jewish-Gentile church had developed practices of shared table fellowship.

Paul "opposed [Cephas] to his face" (κατὰ πρόσωπον αὐτῷ ἀντέστην) — a confrontation in the public assembly, not a private rebuke. The publicity was deliberate: because Peter's behavior was influencing the whole community, the correction had to be equally public. "Because he stood condemned" (ὅτι κατεγνωσμένος ἦν) — Peter had already condemned himself by the inconsistency between his behavior and his stated convictions.
12:
The eating practices at the center of the dispute: Jewish purity laws required that a faithful Jew not eat food that might have been prepared without regard for kashrut (dietary laws), and many Jews extended this to avoiding table fellowship with Gentiles altogether to prevent inadvertent impurity. Peter, before certain observers arrived, "used to eat with the Gentiles" (συνήσθιεν) — imperfect tense, indicating habitual practice. When "people came from James," he began to draw back.

The "people from James" probably brought no formal prohibition but represented the expectations of the Jerusalem community, where maintaining credibility among non-Christian Jews was a pastoral concern (Acts 21:20-21). Peter's withdrawal was a social calculation: protecting his reputation with the Jerusalem Torah-faithful at the cost of integrity toward the Gentile believers.
13: The social dynamics of the incident: Peter's withdrawal triggered a cascade — "the other Jews joined him in his hypocrisy, so that by their hypocrisy even Barnabas was led astray." The Greek ὑπόκρισις (hypocrisy) means "playing a role" or "acting" — performing a version of oneself that does not match reality. The problem was not that the Jewish Christians were wrong to observe food laws privately; the problem was that their withdrawal from Gentile table-fellowship communicated that Gentile believers were unclean — a denial of the gospel's claim that Gentiles are fully accepted in Christ. Even Barnabas, Paul's trusted colleague, was swept along by the social pressure.
14: Paul's confrontation centers on a specific inconsistency: Peter (and the other Jewish Christians) had been living ἐθνικῶς ("like a Gentile") — that is, without Torah-observance as the boundary marker of covenant identity — but under social pressure was now requiring Gentiles to ἰουδαΐζειν ("live like Jews," adopt Jewish customs). The word Ioudaizein described Gentile adoption of Jewish practices; it occurs in Esther 8:17 (LXX) where Gentiles "became Jews" out of fear. Paul's point: you cannot live without Torah's social boundaries yourself and simultaneously demand that Gentile believers adopt them as a condition of full fellowship.
15: "Jews by birth and not sinful Gentiles" — ἁμαρτωλοὶ ἐξ ἐθνῶν. In Jewish sociological shorthand, Gentiles were by definition "sinners" because they lived outside the Torah's covenant framework, with no mechanism for atonement or covenant membership. This was not a moral judgment about individual Gentile character but a structural designation: outside the law, outside the covenant, outside the means of rectification. Paul acknowledges the Jewish-insider perspective before dismantling it.
16:
This verse introduces "justification" (δικαιοσύνη/δικαιόω) — the forensic term for being declared righteous before God's tribunal — into Galatians for the first time. The courtroom background is important: in Jewish thought, the final judgment would determine who is in the right (who is "justified") before God. Paul's argument is that no one achieves this verdict through law-observance. The law pronounces the standard; it does not produce the standing. The triple repetition of "works of the law" (ἔργα νόμου) in this verse emphasizes that Paul's argument is not about moral effort in general but specifically about Torah observance as the basis for covenantal standing.

The phrase "faith in Jesus Christ" translates the contested ἐκ πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — debated whether it means "faith in Christ" (objective genitive, our faith directed at Christ) or "the faithfulness of Christ" (subjective genitive, Christ's own covenant faithfulness). The MKT follows the objective genitive reading; both are grammatically defensible.
17: Paul addresses a potential objection: if Jews abandon Torah observance as the boundary of covenant identity and stand alongside Gentile "sinners" in Christ, does Christ then "promote sin" by erasing the distinction? The objection assumes that the Jewish-Gentile distinction was itself a moral distinction, not merely a covenantal one. Paul's "absolutely not" (μὴ γένοιτο) — the strongest denial formula in his letters — rejects the premise: standing alongside Gentiles in Christ does not make Jews sinners; it reveals that the Gentile condition was never primarily one of moral failure but of covenant exclusion, now overcome in Christ.
18: "If I rebuild what I destroyed" — Paul's compressed argument: if he reverts to law-observance as the boundary of covenant standing (rebuilding the wall between Jew and Gentile he tore down through the gospel), he declares himself a transgressor — because the rebuilding would imply that abandoning Torah observance was wrong in the first place. The irony: the very attempt to re-establish law-keeping as the condition of righteousness is itself a violation of the covenant of grace, making the rebuilder the transgressor.
19: "Through the law I died to the law" — Paul's compressed statement of a complex reality that Romans 7 unpacks at length. The law itself, when fully applied, brings death to any hope of achieving covenant standing through performance — it is the law that condemns, and the condemnation produces death. But this death is the death necessary to live for God, because it evacuates the self-reliant posture the law required. Paul's biographical account and his theological argument converge: his conversion involved dying to the identity constructed around Torah-zeal.
20:
"I have been crucified with Christ" — the perfect passive συνεσταύρωμαι indicates an action that occurred in the past (at conversion/baptism) with ongoing present effect: Paul remains in the condition of one who has been co-crucified with Christ. This is not metaphor but theological realism: in Paul's understanding, the believer's identification with Christ's death was so complete that his death was, in some sense, their death — the mechanism by which the old self-construction was permanently ended.

"The life I now live in the body, I live by faith in the Son of God" — ζῶ δὲ οὐκέτι ἐγώ, ζῇ δὲ ἐν ἐμοὶ Χριστός. The paradox: Paul lives, yet not he; Christ lives in him. This is the lived expression of the two-ages framework — the old Adamic existence is ended, a new existence defined by indwelling Christ has begun, while the biological reality of "life in the body" continues. The phrase "in the body" (ἐν σαρκί) refers to Paul's ongoing mortal existence, not a negative evaluation of embodiment.
21: "I do not set aside the grace of God" — the word ἀθετῶ means to annul, invalidate, or treat as void; it is a legal term. The gospel Paul preaches does not annul grace; it is the grace of God. To add law-observance as a condition of full covenant standing would be to set aside grace — to declare that Christ's death was not sufficient. The final statement is devastating: "if righteousness could be gained through the law, Christ died for nothing" (δωρεάν, "as a free gift that serves no purpose, pointlessly"). The logical consequence of the Judaizer position is the nullification of the cross.
